title: Ceasefire Extension Tempers Hormuz Crisis as Taiwan Frets Over US Arms Delay
subhead: A 60-day ceasefire extension between the US and Iran has eased immediate tensions in the Strait of Hormuz, but key questions remain unanswered — including whether Iran will retain de facto control of the waterway. Meanwhile, delays in a $14 billion US arms sale to Taiwan and Tomahawk shipments to Japan are raising doubts about US reliability in the Indo-Pacific.

sections.heading: US-Iran Ceasefire Extension Calms Markets, Not Strategic Questions
sections.body:
US and Iranian negotiators reached a 60-day memorandum of understanding to extend their fragile ceasefire and begin negotiations over Iran's nuclear program, according to CNBC reporting citing US sources. Brent crude fell 58 cents to close at $93.71 per barrel on Thursday after the news broke, erasing earlier gains triggered by fresh military strikes.

The extension comes after Iran launched ballistic missiles toward Kuwait on Thursday, which US Central Command said were successfully intercepted. The attack followed US strikes against an Iranian military site believed to threaten American troops and commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Several Iranian drones were also intercepted, CNBC reported.

Iranian state television claimed Wednesday that Tehran had agreed in a draft MOU to reopen the Strait of Hormuz to prewar commercial traffic levels — but under joint Iran-Oman management. The White House dismissed that report as a 'complete fabrication,' with Trump stating that 'no nation will control shipping through the strait.' Yet former Biden energy advisor Amos Hochstein told CNBC that regional leaders already believe Iran has effectively taken control of Hormuz regardless of what any agreement says.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Wednesday that Trump prefers diplomacy and will give talks 'every chance to succeed.' But the fundamental gaps between US demands (uranium destruction, toll-free passage) and Iran's position remain unresolved. Citigroup noted that oil markets are finding firmer footing as investors price out worst-case scenarios — but cautioned that uncertainty over timing is keeping central banks on alert for energy-driven inflation risks.
sections.whyItMatters: The extension buys time but does not resolve the core dispute. Iran's claim to control Hormuz traffic, even if denied by Washington, reflects a reality on the ground: Tehran has demonstrated it can disrupt global oil flows at will. Whether the 60-day period produces a lasting agreement or simply delays the next confrontation depends on whether either side is willing to make genuine concessions — something neither has shown willingness to do.

sections.heading: Taiwan Arms Sale Delay Fuels Doubts About US Commitment
sections.body:
A proposed $14 billion US arms sale to Taiwan remains in limbo, with President Trump stating that the package is 'under consideration' and suggesting it could be used as a 'negotiating chip' with Beijing, according to an ISW analysis citing Reuters. The package includes Patriot surface-to-air missile systems, NASAMS, and interceptor munitions — critical components for Taiwan's planned integrated air and missile defense network, known as the 'T-dome.'

Taiwanese President William Lai has made T-dome a key priority of his administration. A robust air defense network is essential for Taiwan to withstand Chinese precision strikes preceding any invasion attempt. But the delay is now being weaponized by KMT-aligned media in Taiwan to cast doubt on US support, with outlets suggesting Trump is bowing to Chinese pressure.

Reuters reported May 22 that no preparations have been made for a call between Trump and Lai, despite Trump saying May 20 that he would speak to Lai about the sale. US and Taiwanese presidents have not publicly held exchanges since Washington severed diplomatic relations with Taipei in 1979. Further delays or cancellation, the ISW notes, would 'have significant deleterious effects on Taiwanese confidence' in the defense partnership.

The timing is sensitive: Taiwan is the only APEC member that does not send its head of government to the annual summit, because Beijing blocks such participation. Yet the PRC allowed Taiwan's Minister Without Portfolio Yang Jen-ni to attend an APEC ministerial meeting in Suzhou on May 22-23 — the most senior Taiwanese official to visit the PRC on official business since 2016, when Beijing cut off exchanges after Tsai Ing-wen's election.
sections.whyItMatters: Taiwan's defense planning depends on timely US weapons deliveries. A delay of several years — as happened with previous packages — would leave a gap in air defense precisely when China is accelerating its military modernization. The political impact may be more immediate: KMT politicians can point to the delay as evidence that Taiwan cannot rely on Washington, strengthening the case for cross-strait accommodation with Beijing.

sections.heading: Japan's Tomahawk Missiles Delayed by Iran War
sections.body:
The United States has warned Japan that a planned delivery of 400 Tomahawk cruise missiles may be delayed by two years due to the Iran war, according to ISW citing Taipei Times. The missiles were initially scheduled for delivery in two shipments completed by 2028; the delay could push completion to 2030.

Japan had already begun incorporating Tomahawks into training and was preparing to place them on its Aegis-equipped JS Chokai destroyer. The 1,600-kilometer range of Tomahawks is critical to Japan's evolving defense concept, which is shifting from territorial defense to offensive counterstrike capabilities that can respond to Chinese or North Korean missile threats.

Defense expert Tatsumi Yuki told Taipei Times that Japan's defense concept, currently under revision, assumed on-time delivery. PRC state media CCTV cited a defense expert saying the delay would affect Japan's defense plans — part of Beijing's broader narrative that the US is an unreliable ally.

The delay comes at a delicate moment for Japan's security policy. Tokyo is revising its National Security Strategy and defense buildup plans, with Tomahawks as a centerpiece. Any gap in capability affects Japan's deterrent posture vis-a-vis China and North Korea.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan is one of America's closest allies and a cornerstone of Indo-Pacific security. Weapon delivery delays undermine confidence in the US-Japan alliance precisely when Tokyo is making the politically difficult shift from pacifism to counterstrike doctrine. Beijing is already exploiting the narrative — the question is whether Japan adjusts its defense plans or recalibrates its assumptions about US reliability.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Robotic Warfare Push Forces Russian Adjustments
sections.body:
CNN reported May 30 that Ukraine's deployment of robotic ground systems is 'redefining the war in Ukraine — and forcing Russia onto the back foot.' The report details how unmanned ground vehicles are increasingly taking on roles previously performed by soldiers, from logistics to reconnaissance to direct combat.

According to Ukraine's General Staff, Russian forces lost 1,430 personnel over the past day, along with two tanks, seven armored combat vehicles, 70 artillery systems, five MLRS, one air defense system, four ground robotic systems, and 1,781 drones. The scale of drone losses — 8,549 kamikaze drones deployed by Russia in a single day, according to Ukrainian officials — illustrates how both sides are saturating the battlefield with autonomous systems.

ISW's May 30 assessment notes that Ukrainian forces continue to strike Russian logistics and command infrastructure. Ukrainian intermediate-range strike capabilities have created what ISW calls 'the start of a new phase of the war,' with systematic attacks on Russian rear areas disrupting Moscow's ability to sustain offensive operations.

The EMPR war report for May 30 documents 291 combat engagements across the front, with the heaviest fighting near Pokrovsk (49 Russian assaults repelled) and in the Huliaipole direction (40 attacks). Ukrainian aviation and artillery struck an enemy command post, three artillery systems, and nine troop concentration areas.
sections.whyItMatters: The war in Ukraine has become a laboratory for autonomous and robotic warfare at a scale no other conflict has matched. As both sides lose thousands of drones per day, the lessons learned are being absorbed by militaries worldwide — including China, Iran, and NATO members. The question is whether Ukraine's technological adaptation can offset Russian advantages in manpower and artillery, or whether the war settles into a grinding attrition that neither side can win decisively.

sections.heading: Gaza Ceasefire at Six Months: Progress Stalled, Needs Unmet
sections.body:
Friday marks six months since Gaza's ceasefire took effect on October 10, 2025 — but the milestone has been overshadowed by the Iran conflict and, according to AP, largely 'lost in the confusion.' While the most intense fighting has stopped, humanitarian conditions remain dire and key provisions of the US 20-point ceasefire plan remain unimplemented.

Aid groups released a scorecard Thursday showing conditions have actually deteriorated since the Iran war began. 'During the first two weeks of March 2026, trucks entering Gaza declined by 80%, and the price of basic goods increased dramatically,' five international organizations reported. Medical evacuations have stalled. Most aid still enters through a single, Israeli-controlled border post.

The biggest unresolved issue: Hamas has not responded to the Board of Peace's disarmament proposal. A US official said Hamas has not been given a definite deadline but 'patience is not unlimited.' Hamas submitted a counter-offer tying any disarmament to guarantees for a sovereign Palestinian state, a halt to Israel's westward expansion in Gaza, and a complete ceasefire — demands Israel has rejected.

AP reports that Israeli attacks have killed 738 people in Gaza in the six months since the ceasefire began. While heaviest fighting has subsided, Israeli forces continue airstrikes and fire on Palestinians near military zones, while militants have carried out shooting attacks on troops.
sections.whyItMatters: The Gaza ceasefire was supposed to demonstrate that the Board of Peace model — stopping bombardment, then addressing underlying issues — could work. Six months in, the underlying issues are no closer to resolution. Hamas remains armed and in control of parts of Gaza. Israel controls 58% of the territory, up from 53% under the original ceasefire map. The Board of Peace has not met again since the US-Israel strike on Iran began. Gaza offers a cautionary lesson for any Iran deal: a ceasefire without a political settlement is just a pause, not an ending.

sections.heading: China's Diplomatic Balancing Act on Taiwan
sections.body:
In an unusual move, the PRC allowed Taiwanese Minister Without Portfolio Yang Jen-ni — also head of Taiwan's Office of Trade Negotiations — to attend an APEC ministerial meeting in Suzhou on May 22-23. Yang is the most senior sitting Taiwanese official to visit the PRC on official business since Beijing cut off official exchanges in 2016 after Tsai Ing-wen's election.

According to ISW, the PRC maintains that cross-strait relations must be based on the '1992 Consensus' — an alleged verbal agreement that Taiwan is part of 'China,' without specifying which government represents China. Tsai, current President Lai, and the DPP do not recognize this consensus.

Beijing's acceptance of a ministerial-level Taiwanese official is an exception to its escalating campaign to constrain Taiwan's international participation. The PRC has blocked Taiwan from the World Health Assembly every year since 2016, pressured countries to exclude or downgrade Taiwan's participation in international organizations, and financially coerced three African countries to close their airspace to block President Lai's state visit to Eswatini.

Yet as APEC host, Beijing chose to allow Yang's participation rather than trigger a crisis. The calculation may reflect Beijing's desire to project stability at a time when the US-China relationship is under strain over Taiwan arms sales and the Iran conflict.
sections.whyItMatters: Beijing's approach to Taiwan is not monolithic. It combines pressure (blocking WHO participation, airspace coercion) with selective engagement (APEC participation, potential summitry). The Yang visit shows Beijing can calibrate — but the underlying coercion continues. Taiwan's international space remains constrained by Chinese vetoes in every multilateral venue where statehood is required.

bottomLine: Saturday, May 31: The 60-day US-Iran ceasefire extension has bought both sides time and calmed oil markets — but it has not resolved the core strategic question of who controls the Strait of Hormuz. Iran claims joint management with Oman; the White House calls that a fabrication. Regional actors tell CNBC the reality is simpler: Iran controls Hormuz now, regardless of what any agreement says. In the Indo-Pacific, two US weapons delays — Taiwan's $14 billion air defense package and Japan's 400 Tomahawk missiles — are being exploited by Beijing to sow doubt about American reliability. Taiwan's KMT-aligned media and China's state broadcasters are amplifying the same message: Washington cannot be counted on. Meanwhile, Ukraine's robotic warfare push is forcing Russia to adapt, and Gaza's six-month ceasefire has produced a stalemate where Hamas remains armed, Israel controls 58% of the territory, and humanitarian conditions have deteriorated since the Iran war began. The connecting thread across these theaters: ceasefires and diplomatic agreements are only as good as the follow-through, and follow-through is in short supply.
